title: Day 22 Interview Prep — 20 Questions
description: Full interview simulation: 20 questions across React, JavaScript, TypeScript, architecture, and performance. Answer out loud. Follow-ups are mandatory practice.

questions.question: Explain the JavaScript event loop in 60 seconds.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Async fundamentals — appears in every frontend loop.
questions.sampleAnswer: JavaScript is single-threaded. Synchronous code runs on the call stack. Async callbacks go to the task queue. Promises and queueMicrotask run as microtasks before the next macrotask. The event loop checks if the stack is empty, drains microtasks, then picks the next macrotask. That is why Promise.then runs before setTimeout(0).
followUps: What runs first: setTimeout or Promise.then?

questions.question: What is a closure? Give a React-relevant example.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Core JS concept tied to hooks and stale closures.
questions.sampleAnswer: A closure is when a function retains access to variables from its creation scope. In React, a debounce hook closes over timer id. A stale closure bug happens when useEffect captures an old count because deps were wrong — the effect function closed over stale state.
followUps: How fix stale closure in useEffect?

questions.question: Implement debounce conceptually — what are the inputs and output behavior?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Can they explain without copying lodash?
questions.sampleAnswer: debounce(fn, ms) returns a wrapper. Each call clears the previous timeout and sets a new one. fn runs only after ms milliseconds of silence. Used for search inputs to avoid firing API on every keystroke.

questions.question: map vs filter vs reduce — when do you use each?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Array fluency for list UIs like Kanban.
questions.sampleAnswer: map transforms each element to a new array — rendering DTOs. filter keeps elements matching a predicate — tasks in Done column. reduce accumulates to a single value — counting tasks per column or building a lookup map.

questions.question: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy of state?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Immutability understanding for Redux and React.
questions.sampleAnswer: Shallow copy duplicates top-level references — {...obj} copies keys but nested objects are still shared. Deep copy clones all levels. React state updates need at least shallow copy at the level you change. Nested cart item update needs {...item, qty: newQty} inside map.

questions.question: Controlled vs uncontrolled components — which do you default to and why?
questions.sampleAnswer: I default to controlled — value from state, onChange updates state. Enables validation, disabled submit, and predictable testing. Uncontrolled with ref is fine for simple file inputs or integrating non-React libraries.

questions.question: Explain useEffect — when do you use it and when do you avoid it?
questions.sampleAnswer: useEffect synchronizes with external systems — APIs, subscriptions, DOM, timers. I avoid it for transforming data that can be computed during render. I avoid it for user events — those belong in handlers. Always specify dependencies; return cleanup for subscriptions.

questions.question: Context vs Redux — how do you decide?
questions.sampleAnswer: Context for low-frequency global reads — theme, locale. Redux Toolkit when many actions, selectors, middleware, time-travel debugging, and multiple slices interact. Neither replaces RTK Query for server cache. I do not put everything in either.

questions.question: What problem does RTK Query solve?
questions.sampleAnswer: RTK Query manages server state — automatic caching, background refetch, tag invalidation, loading and error flags per endpoint. It deduplicates in-flight requests. I pick it when already on Redux; React Query when I want standalone server state without Redux.

questions.question: How do protected routes work in React Router?
questions.sampleAnswer: A ProtectedRoute reads auth state — Context or Redux. If authenticated, render Outlet or children. If not, Navigate to /login with replace. Token in memory or localStorage; validate on app init. Never rely on hiding links alone — guard the route.

questions.question: interface vs type in TypeScript — when do you use each?
questions.sampleAnswer: Both work for React props. I use interface for object shapes that may be extended — API models, component props. I use type for unions, intersections, and mapped types — ThemePreference = 'light' | 'dark'. Consistency across the codebase matters more than the choice.

questions.question: How do you type useParams for a route like /tasks/:taskId?
questions.sampleAnswer: const { taskId } = useParams<{ taskId: string }>(). taskId is string | undefined — params can be missing. I guard: if (!taskId) return <NotFound />. For strict apps, validate with Zod or redirect.

questions.question: How do you type an API response for a Kanban task list?
questions.sampleAnswer: I define TaskApiResponse matching JSON from the server, then map to Task UI type if shapes differ. status: 'todo' | 'in_progress' | 'done' as union. Keep types in types/task.ts — never inline in components.

questions.question: Describe your ideal folder structure for a medium React app.
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Code review and project discussion readiness.
questions.sampleAnswer: src/features/kanban/ with components, hooks, api, types per feature. src/components/ui for shared atoms. src/hooks for cross-feature hooks. src/routes for route definitions. Colocate what changes together. Avoid dumping everything in components/ with 40 files flat.

questions.question: You have 90 minutes to build a Kanban board. Walk me through your first 10 minutes.
questions.sampleAnswer: Minutes 1–3: clarify — move via buttons OK? API provided? Minutes 4–5: sketch Task type and three columns. Minutes 6–8: scaffold folders and static UI with hardcoded tasks. Minutes 9–10: wire add task locally. I narrate so the interviewer can follow and redirect early.
followUps: What do you cut if 20 minutes left?

questions.question: When do you apply React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Performance — Week 3 Day 20, not premature optimization.
keyPoints: useCallback for stable ref to memo child
questions.sampleAnswer: I profile first — React DevTools Profiler. React.memo when a pure child re-renders often with same props. useMemo for expensive filtering of large lists. useCallback when passing handlers to memoized children. I do not wrap every component — memo has cost too.

questions.question: Your Kanban list is slow with 500 tasks. What do you check?
questions.sampleAnswer: Profiler for hot components. Check keys are stable ids. Virtualize with react-window if rendering 500 DOM nodes. Ensure context value is memoized. Filter columns with useMemo from single source array. Avoid passing new inline objects as props each render.

questions.question: Code review: a teammate uses any for all API responses. Your comment?
questions.sampleAnswer: I ask for interfaces matching API contract — catches field renames at compile time. Suggest unknown + type guard or Zod for runtime safety. Offer to pair on types/task.ts. Frame as preventing production undefined crashes, not criticism.

questions.question: What accessibility checks do you run before submitting machine coding?
questions.whatInterviewerTests: Code review checklist — a11y often forgotten.
questions.sampleAnswer: Buttons are button elements with accessible names. Form inputs have labels. Error messages use role=alert or aria-live. Column move actions are keyboard reachable. Color contrast meets WCAG for text. I tab through the flow once before saying done.

questions.question: Why should reusable components matter in a 90-minute interview build?
questions.sampleAnswer: I extract TaskCard and ColumnShell when used three times — saves time and shows I think beyond the demo. I do not build a full design system — that is over-engineering. One shared Button and Card proves reusability without slowing MVP.
redFlags: Copy-paste JSX three times is faster so skip components
